narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average among past students, along with your Year 1 school decile of 5—right around the average—and your middle family income bracket. You've also indicated plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students in the dataset we draw from. The admissions model, designed to predict first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 42.7% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction. Your undergraduate GPA was the most powerful positive force, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass the bar, likely because it signals solid academic preparation beyond what's typical. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 24 had the strongest pull in the opposite direction, significantly increasing the predicted risk of failure compared to the average score of nearly 37, making it a major hurdle in the model's eyes. Your Year 1 decile ranking provided a milder positive nudge toward passing, helping a bit but not enough to offset the others. Overall, these elements tipped the scales toward concern, even as your GPA shone brightly. While your full-time status and background align with successful applicants, the model saw your LSAT as a standout weakness relative to peers who passed. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, as that single change could dramatically shift your predicted odds. You might also gather letters highlighting your GPA strengths or bar prep potential. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed—your solid foundation gives you a great starting point. Keep pushing; law school doors can reopen with targeted steps.
